default_annotator.report_text:
This document is used in phishing campaigns to execute a series of scripting languages Attackers use email attachments containing malicious files to execute a series of scripting languages containing multiple obfuscation mechanisms and advanced techniques to bypass traditional security mechanisms. The use of script languages to execute malicious code.
FIN7 uses techniques such as file obfuscation to avoid detection by traditional security tools.
The document contains messages enticing the user to click on an embedded object that executes scripts Attackers rely on user actions to open malicious files or click on embedded objects, triggering the malware execution.
This malware is then used to steal passwords from popular browsers and mail clients Attackers steal saved login credentials from web browsers, gaining access to accounts.
FIN7's malware uses legitimate web protocols such as HTTP/HTTPS to communicate with its command-and-control servers.
a new infection vector technique using an RTF document containing an embedded JavaScript OLE object Attackers deliver phishing emails containing malicious attachments, in this case, an RTF document embedded with a JavaScript OLE object.
When clicked it launches an infection chain The attack relies on the user clicking the embedded OLE object to trigger the infection chain.
an infection chain made up of JavaScript, and a Knal shellcode payload Execution of JavaScript from the embedded OLE object to begin the infection process.
that makes use of DNS to load additional shellcode from a remote command and control server Using DNS as a channel to communicate with the command and control (C2) server and load additional shellcode.
this new document variant that makes use of an LNK embedded OLE object The attack starts with a spear-phishing email containing a malicious document with an LNK embedded OLE object.
which extracts a JavaScript bot from a document object, The JavaScript bot is extracted and executed as part of the infection chain.
injects a stealer DLL in memory The stealer DLL is injected directly into memory using PowerShell, allowing the malware to run in the context of legitimate processes.
PowerShell is used to inject the stealer DLL into memory, a key part of the execution chain.
The dropper variant that we encountered makes use of an LNK Kle to execute wscript.exe The dropper leverages a crafted LNK file to initiate the execution of malicious code on the victim's machine, embedded within the Word document.
the beginning of the JavaScript chain The JavaScript chain is used for the next stage of execution
from a word document object The victim likely opens a malicious Word document object, initiating the infection process.
